title: CELPIP-General Practice Exam — Advanced
notes: Original practice content written in CELPIP-General style for development/seed purposes. Audio files referenced by 'audioAsset' must be added under assets/audio/. While real audio is unavailable, 'audioTranscript' can drive a text-to-speech fallback or on-screen mock.

sections.title: Listening
sections.instructions: You will hear each recording ONCE. You cannot pause, rewind, or replay. Answer the questions as you listen.

questions.audioTranscript: Woman: Hi, I'm calling about the patio set I ordered last week. The chairs arrived, but the table is missing two of its legs and the glass top has a long crack across it. Man: I'm very sorry to hear that. Let me pull up your order. Would you prefer a full replacement of the table, or a refund for that portion of the order? Woman: Honestly, I'd rather just get a working table. We have guests this weekend, so the timing is tight. Man: Understood. I can ship a replacement table today with overnight delivery at no charge, and I'll add a fifteen percent credit for the inconvenience. Woman: That works, as long as it actually arrives before Saturday. Man: It will. I'll email you the new tracking number within the hour.
questions.prompt: What is the woman's main concern about resolving the problem?
options: Getting the largest possible refund
Receiving a usable table before the weekend
Returning the chairs that already arrived
Speaking with a different representative

questions.prompt: What does the man offer in addition to the replacement?
options: A full refund and free chairs
A discount on a future order
Overnight shipping and a fifteen percent credit
An extended warranty on the patio set

questions.prompt: What will the man do within the hour?
options: Call the woman back to confirm
Send a new tracking number by email
Process a partial refund
Schedule a technician visit

questions.audioTranscript: City officials announced today that the downtown transit corridor will undergo a two-year redevelopment beginning this autumn. The plan replaces the aging bus loop with a dedicated light-rail line intended to cut average commute times by roughly twenty percent. Critics argue the projected budget of four hundred million dollars underestimates the disruption to local businesses during construction. The transit authority counters that temporary relief grants and a phased schedule will limit closures to one block at a time. A public consultation period opens next month, after which the final design will be submitted for council approval.
questions.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the redevelopment project?
options: To expand parking near the downtown core
To replace the bus loop with a light-rail line
To reduce the city's overall transit budget
To relocate local businesses out of downtown

questions.prompt: What is the main objection raised by critics?
options: The light rail will be too slow
The budget underestimates disruption to businesses
The project will not reduce commute times
Public consultation was skipped entirely

questions.prompt: According to the transit authority, how will disruption be limited?
options: By closing the entire corridor at once to finish faster
By cancelling the project if costs rise
By using relief grants and closing one block at a time
By postponing construction until next year

questions.audioTranscript: Some economists maintain that remote work permanently reshapes urban centres. They point to declining office occupancy and the migration of skilled workers toward smaller, more affordable cities. Others remain skeptical, noting that collaboration and mentorship suffer without shared physical space, and that many firms are quietly reintroducing in-person requirements. A more measured view suggests the outcome is hybrid by necessity: companies will retain smaller offices as hubs for periodic gathering rather than daily attendance, trading square footage for flexibility while preserving the social fabric that fully remote arrangements tend to erode.
questions.prompt: What does the speaker present as the 'more measured view'?
options: Remote work will completely replace offices
Offices will return to full daily attendance
A hybrid model with smaller offices used as hubs
Skilled workers will return to large cities

questions.prompt: Why are some observers skeptical of fully remote work?
options: It increases office occupancy
It harms collaboration and mentorship
It makes cities more affordable
It eliminates the need for hybrid schedules

sections.title: Reading
sections.instructions: Read each passage and answer the questions. You may go back and review within this section.

questions.passage:
Dear Mr. Alvarado,

Thank you for your application for the Site Coordinator position. After reviewing your portfolio, the committee was impressed by your experience managing multi-trade schedules. We would like to invite you to a second-round interview, which will focus on a practical scheduling scenario rather than general questions. Please bring a recent project timeline you have personally prepared. The interview is set for Thursday at 10:00 a.m. at our Surrey office. If this time does not suit you, reply by Tuesday and we will arrange an alternative.

Warm regards,
Dana Whitford, Hiring Lead
questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of this email?
options: To reject the applicant's portfolio
To invite the applicant to a second interview
To request a new job application
To confirm a salary offer

questions.passage: (Refer to the email from Dana Whitford above.)
questions.prompt: What is the applicant asked to bring?
options: A list of references
A signed contract
A project timeline he personally prepared
A copy of his diploma

questions.passage: (Refer to the email from Dana Whitford above.)
questions.prompt: What should the applicant do if the proposed time does not work?
options: Decline the position
Arrive at a different office
Reply by Tuesday to arrange another time
Wait for a phone call

questions.passage: Predictive maintenance differs from preventive maintenance in a crucial way. Preventive maintenance relies on fixed schedules: a part is serviced or replaced after a set interval, regardless of its actual condition. Predictive maintenance, by contrast, uses condition-monitoring data—vibration analysis, thermography, and ultrasound, among others—to estimate the remaining useful life of equipment and intervene only when measurable indicators suggest impending failure. This approach can reduce unnecessary part replacement and minimize unplanned downtime, but it requires upfront investment in sensors, trained analysts, and data infrastructure. For facilities with critical, high-cost assets, the return on that investment is typically realized within the first two years of deployment.
questions.prompt: How does predictive maintenance mainly differ from preventive maintenance?
options: It replaces parts on a fixed schedule
It intervenes based on condition-monitoring data
It eliminates the need for any sensors
It requires no upfront investment

questions.passage: (Refer to the passage on predictive maintenance above.)
questions.prompt: Which is named as a condition-monitoring technique?
options: Fixed-interval scheduling
Thermography
Annual inspection
Manual inventory counting

questions.passage: (Refer to the passage on predictive maintenance above.)
questions.prompt: For which facilities is the investment said to pay off soonest?
options: Those with low-cost, non-critical assets
Those that avoid using sensors
Those with critical, high-cost assets
Those that rely only on fixed schedules

questions.passage:
Opinion A: Universities should prioritize practical, job-ready skills, since graduates face a competitive labour market and employers increasingly value demonstrable competencies over abstract theory.

Opinion B: Universities exist to cultivate critical thinking and intellectual breadth; narrowing curricula to immediate employability produces narrow specialists who struggle to adapt as industries evolve.
questions.prompt: What is the central tension between the two opinions?
options: Whether universities should charge tuition
Whether to emphasize job-ready skills or intellectual breadth
Whether employers should fund universities
Whether students should attend university at all

questions.passage: (Refer to Opinions A and B above.)
questions.prompt: What concern does Opinion B raise about prioritizing employability?
options: It makes tuition unaffordable
It produces specialists who may struggle to adapt
It reduces the number of graduates
It discourages employers from hiring

sections.title: Writing
sections.instructions: Complete both tasks. Aim for 150-200 words each. Manage your time across the two tasks.

rubricHints: Clear purpose and appropriate professional tone
Logical organization with a clear opening and closing
Range and accuracy of vocabulary and grammar

questions.prompt:
Your city is considering how to spend a limited infrastructure budget. A survey asks residents to choose ONE of the following options and explain their choice:

Option A: Invest in expanding public transit (more bus routes and a new light-rail line).

Option B: Invest in road improvements (repaving, new lanes, and better intersections).

Choose ONE option. Explain your choice with specific reasons and examples. State clearly why you did not choose the other option.
rubricHints: A clear position stated and maintained
Acknowledgement of why the other option was rejected

sections.title: Speaking
sections.instructions: You will have preparation time, then recording will begin automatically. Speak until the time runs out. You cannot re-record.

questions.prompt: A friend has been offered a two-year job contract in another country at a good salary but is unsure whether to accept. Give your friend advice about what to consider before deciding.
Describe a time when you had to solve a difficult problem at work or school. What was the situation, and how did you handle it?
Look at the picture. Describe the scene in as much detail as you can for someone who cannot see it. Mention the people, their actions, and the setting.
Look again at the same picture. Predict what you think will happen next, and explain why you think so.

You are choosing equipment for a new business. Compare two options: a higher-cost model with advanced features, or a lower-cost model that covers the basics. Choose one and persuade your business partner to agree with you.

You promised to help a colleague move on Saturday, but your manager has now scheduled an important deadline for the same day. You can speak to either your colleague or your manager. Choose one, explain the situation, and propose a solution.
Some people believe companies should be required to allow employees to work remotely at least part of the week. Do you agree or disagree? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
Look at the picture. Something unusual is happening. Call a friend and describe what you see, including why it is surprising or out of the ordinary.
